heading: Woot! Change of tooling.
comments:
After a disagreement between black and the
reorder-python-imports utility I have abandoned
both, along with flake8, in favour of ruff.

It's a shame the Python world can't get its act
together, but if it means people will build us

abandon black, so it's back for now and we'll see
where we end up.

heading: Feature request
comments:
It would be at least marginally useful to be able to
make repeated entries, by default to the same
project. This could be implemented by a "do another"
checkbox or similar, avoiding the need to rerun the
program repeatedly.

heading: Home directory field?
comments:
Adding a `home_dir` (or similarly named) attribute
would allow location of the authoritative code
for the project.

It would also be helpful to adopt a directory
naming convention that allowed multiple versions
of the code to exist. For example, using a plus
sign as a prefix indicator, for the `hu` project
we would recognise `hu+1`, `hu+hotfix-23` and
`hu+next-release` as being based on the project.

Superficially it's attractive, but some solid use
cases would avoid wasted efforts.

heading: Release discipline ...
comments:
... is shitty on this project. Primarily because I
have not yet released anything yet, but this note
is a stake in the ground that the process needs to
work as well as Textual's appears to.

heading: Argument handling
comments:
It would be nice to have some convenient way to
indicate on the command line which project the
command was supposed to work on - though always
falling back to whatver default the user has
established if no database (can I find a more
user-friendly term?)

Something simple, since I'd hope most accesses
would be via TUIs, but good enough for the odd
management comand like `db_create`.

QUESTION: who says functions like `db_create` are
best done from the command line? It would be good
to restrict the availability of the TUI but there
seems no good reason why it couldn't be fitted
into a TUI framework.

heading: Starting with the layout
comments:
The new maintain.py program is a
placeholder skeleton to make sure
that basic layout ideas are sound
step by step. Consequently the
starting model for the layout has
literal Placeholder objects as its
second-level components.

It adopts my usual convention of
logging both the DOM structure and
its associated CSS trees on a click.

heading: Generic framework operations
comments:
Guess what - the NoteForm is a dynamic screen,
whose `status` pane (i.e. the `info` and `operations`
containers) should therefore be populated and
managed by (a) method(s) of the class on which it
is operating.

This implies that the time to populate those
containers is when an object type is selected.

That should be enough to start fleshing out some
logic once some actual controls are in place.
Also note that the smart way to do this is to
inherit from Document and add methods to interact
with the framework, then have local documents
inherit from that. The default methods would simply
announce that this document type could not be
maintained.

heading: Could this design eat itself?
comments:
I was toying with the idea of putting the operation
definitions in the database. I could then use the
program to edit the program. Very meta - but not at
all impossible!

heading: Massive screwup on layout, or what?
comments:
OK, so I wrote logic to show the NoteForm when an
object type had been selected, or otherwise to show
a splash screen.

Starting it with no object type now occupies the
whole freaking screen with the splash, blotting out
all other components, when the program starts.

heading: Bringing tagset into the notes fold
comments:
There's an expressed need for a combo box in the
Textual toolkit, which they seem to envisage as a
refinement of the current `Select` object, which
to me seems a little clumsy.

Consequently I'm going to polish up the tagset
repository, make the demo more easily accessible
as a virtualenv entry point and see how usable
it is in the notes project.

heading: Mounting does what add_child should
comments:
It really is remarkably difficult to find out from
the `textual` documentation how to add widgets to
a container dynamically.

The asnwer, as suggested by the heading, is `mount()`.

heading: Need a clean search interface
comments:
At present the way the `TagSet.update` method works
is by optionally creating a new set of members (this
make creation of new TagSets simpler), and then
selecting those elements that meet the search
criteria.

While this works, it means that for the widget to
operate on collections of records we should really
apply the filter at the point of retrieval and not
select all candidates then strip the unwanted ones.

This still means that we are potentially revtrieving far more items than we can ever
display at once, which is a separate problem.

heading: Not quite so easy for Selectors, however
comments:
The assumption behind a selector is that every
value must appear either in the selected or the
unselected set. To maintain proper function it
would seem necessary to retrieve both sets with
two filters: one is the standard current filtering,
and the other is a limit to ensure we only retrieve
what needs to be displayed.

However this would mean responding to scrolling by
adding an appropriate offset - and continuous scrolling
would then become somewhat tedious.

For now I intend to ignore these issues, but they
are real and may at some stage need to be be taken seriously.

heading: Progress on light dimming
comments:
OK, I've got this single-channel LED dimmer, as per
https://www.zigbee2mqtt.io/devices/TS0501B.html, but
the simplistic approach to integrating it doesn't
appear to give access to the state, so I have at
present to rely on the built in increase and
decrease operations which are insufficiently
granular.

Yes, this is a new project.

heading: Webhooks could be useful
comments:
According to https://www.home-assistant.io/docs/automation/trigger/#webhook-trigger a URL of the form <HA>/apps/webhook/<hook-id> will act as an automation trigger once `hook-id` is registered
as a trigger of type webhook:

```
automation:
  triggers:
    - trigger: webhook
      webhook_id: "some_hook_id"
      allowed_methods:
        - POST
        - PUT
      local_only: true
```

heading: Named Ranges
comments:
Both the workbook as a whole and each sheet has a
defined_names property that lists the global or
local ranges. I haven't investigated name clashes.

This shows that multiple ranges are collected in a
comma-separated string in the range's `attr_text`
attribute. In the "ExcelCreated" spreadsheet there
are two global names ("Parameters" and "MultiRange"), and one sheet-local one, "Local."

Here's what they look like:

```
workbook.defined_names.keys()workbook.defined_names
{'MultiRange': <openpyxl.workbook.defined_name.DefinedName object>
Parameters:
name='MultiRange', comment=None, customMenu=None, description=None, help=None, statusBar=None, localSheetId=None, hidden=None, function=None, vbProcedure=None, xlm=None, functionGroupId=None, shortcutKey=None, publishToServer=None, workbookParameter=None, attr_text='Sheet1!$D$4:$E$14,Sheet1!$G$4:$H$14,Sheet1!$J$4,_xlfn.ANCHORARRAY(Sheet1!$J$4:$K$14)', 'Parameters': <openpyxl.workbook.defined_name.DefinedName object>
Parameters:
name='Parameters', comment=None, customMenu=None, description=None, help=None, statusBar=None, localSheetId=None, hidden=None, function=None, vbProcedure=None, xlm=None, functionGroupId=None, shortcutKey=None, publishToServer=None, workbookParameter=None, attr_text='Sheet1!$A$1:$B$14'}

s = workbook.get_sheet_by_name('Sheet1')
s.defined_names
{'Local': <openpyxl.workbook.defined_name.DefinedName object>
Parameters:
name='Local', comment='Comment: just for this sheet', customMenu=None, description=None, help=None, statusBar=None, localSheetId=0, hidden=None, function=None, vbProcedure=None, xlm=None, functionGroupId=None, shortcutKey=None, publishToServer=None, workbookParameter=None, attr_text='Sheet1!$A$1'}
```

heading: Cell ranges
comments:
Subscription a sheet with an Excel-like range
string conveniently returns a tuple of rows,
each row being a tuple of cells. That seems to work really nicely.

heading: Common Table Expressions (CTEs)
comments:
TIL that a CTE allows you to factor out a query,
give it a name and re-use it efficiently multiple
times within the same query.

More at https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/cte-in-sql/

heading: Should definitely think about uv
comments:
I now have ~1.5GB of poetry venvs and growing.
They are not in any way managed. If the venv
disappears then the entry points from it also
stop working.

[This question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/79118841/how-to-migrate-from-poetry-to-uv-package-manager)
indicates possible migration paths.
Start with something that actually uses entry
points, like quicksite.

heading: The hubris package lives: vectors
comments:
For convenience, however, I will continue to document it here until I wrap shotlnds and mothball it.

A 1 x n vector appears as a single-element list.
An n x 1 vector appears as a list of single-element lists.
Both will be flattened to a simple list.

heading: Automating branch push targets
comments:
To have all repos automatically associate with the
same branch on remote repositories, apply this
git settings change.

`git config --global push.autoSetupRemote true`

heading: Why I switched to zsh
comments:
I switched to zsh beacuse it seemed better than bash. But what I hadn't realised (until a desire to install [kitten](https://kitten.small-web.org/) arose) that I was judging bash based on the c.2007 release I'd been using from the MacOS /usr/bin/bash.

I've now run `curl -sL https://kittens.small-web.org/install | bash` as recommended, and my bash version has jumped from 3.2.57 to 5.2.37, a somewhat odd numerological coincidence. How much of an improvement this might be I am unable to say, as I became a zsh user a few months after Apple switched to it as the default MacOS shell, so I no longer use bash.

heading: The `filescan` project joins notes
comments:
This is the first project I haven't needed to create manually in MongoDB Compass, so I'm a happy chap.

Filescan is now running irregularly. It would make sense to sync with a remote filestore (providing backup) and then analyse the remote copy, detaching that task from my laptop.

Being able to download a copy of the database to use in local querying would perhaps be interesting.

heading: Archive records being incorrectly formulated
comments:
This is a pain. In the `test_archive` routine I find
that the `Archive` objects don't appear to be
created correctly.

When tracing the execution of the `archive_record`
function I confirmed that the object has a `runlog`
attribute immediately after creation.

The Archive object is at 0x103d72780 and the RunLog
object at 0x103d09a00, implying it was created a
little earlier.

I then discovered that `db.session.execute(select(Archive)).all()` returns a list, not of Archive objecgts, but a list of tuples whose first (and only) elements
are Archive objects.

Clearly I need to understand more about what is
going on here!

heading: Bogus Archive rows being created
comments:
After creating a new database and running filescan
once (revealing 98 new files) I discovered 19 rows
in the RunLog table (with PKs starting at 2, which
I find odd, but :shrug:), with ascending `when_run`
values, all but the last having null `when_finished`
values.

A first glance suggests that a new row is being
created for each directory encountered.

heading: Recasting as an object that can render when required
comments:
Rather than having the Form be a container, Will
McGugan suggested that I consider having it be
an object in its own right with a method that
produces a renderable widget.

So I'm currently trying to model that, and getting
tangled up in class hierarchies.

heading: Difficulties getting uv to package correctly
comments:
At present I'm still looking for the uv equivalent of
```
packages = [
    { include = "textual_forms", from = "src" },
]
```

which causes poetry to build correctly. uv, however,
continues to include everything from the project's
base directory!

heading: Getting there, but slowly
comments:
I'd forgotten how slow the early phases of design
can be, having not built anything of significance
for some time.

Just the same, things are coming together.
The latest breakthrough was to have the form
widgets simply be extended versions of the widget
their field requires, which means they retain all
the expected behaviour of the component.

This has reduced the code considerably, always a
good sign, and highlighted a problem creating
the widgets inside the field's `__init__` - I
was calling `field.default_value` but I've
implemented the method on the widgets!

heading: How to hash for an ObjectId
comments:
If you want determinism, use some unique content string hashed to 12 bytes as an argument to the `bson.obnjectid.ObjectId` instantiation.

```
>>> h = shake_128("hello, world!".encode("utf-8")).digest(12)
>>> h
b'f\x022\xdflt\xad\x19\xed\x93q\xda'
>>> ObjectId(h)
ObjectId('660232df6c74ad19ed9371da')
```

heading: `uv pip` can mislead the unwary
comments:
I just spent a ton of time trying to work out why `uv pip install --editable ../textual-forms`
